text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Researchers with the Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine examined all research studies published from 1950 to March of 2019 that evaluated the effect of eggs on blood cholesterol levels.","claimType":"methodological","quote":"Researchers with the Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine examined all research studies published from 1950 to March of 2019 that evaluated the effect of eggs on blood cholesterol levels.","entailment":"The quote explicitly states that the researchers performed an examination of all studies within that date range evaluating eggs' effect on blood cholesterol.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"1950 to March 2019","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"The percentage of industry-funded studies increased over time, from 0 percent in the 1950s to 60 percent in 2010-2019.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The percentage of industry-funded studies increased over time, from 0 percent in the 1950s to 60 percent in 2010-2019.","entailment":"The quote provides the observed change in proportion of industry-funded studies across the specified periods.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"percentage of industry-funded studies","timeframe":"1950s to 2010-2019","quantifiers":"0 percent to 60 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Overall, more than 85 percent of the studies—whether funded by industry or not—showed that eggs have unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Overall, more than 85 percent of the studies—whether funded by industry or not—showed that eggs have unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol.","entailment":"The quote reports the proportion of studies finding eggs to have unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"more than 85 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Approximately half (49 percent) of industry-funded intervention studies reported conclusions that were discordant with actual study results, compared with 13 percent of non-industry-funded trials.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Approximately half (49 percent) of industry-funded intervention studies reported conclusions that were discordant with actual study results, compared with 13 percent of non-industry-funded trials.","entailment":"The quote gives the percentages of discordant conclusions for industry-funded versus non-industry-funded intervention studies.","population":"","intervention":"industry-funded intervention studies","comparator":"non-industry-funded trials","outcome":"conclusions discordant with actual study results","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"49 percent vs 13 percent","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"In one 2014 study in college freshmen, the addition of two eggs at breakfast, five days a week over 14 weeks, was associated with a mean LDL cholesterol increase of 15 mg/dL.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"In one 2014 study in college freshmen, the addition of two eggs at breakfast, five days a week over 14 weeks, was associated with a mean LDL cholesterol increase of 15 mg/dL.","entailment":"The quote describes the observed mean increase in LDL cholesterol linked to the specified egg consumption regimen.","population":"college freshmen","intervention":"two eggs at breakfast, five days a week over 14 weeks","comparator":"","outcome":"mean LDL cholesterol increase","timeframe":"14 weeks","quantifiers":"15 mg/dL","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"The cholesterol change did not reach statistical significance, meaning that there was at least a 5 percent chance that the cholesterol rise could have been due to chance alone.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The cholesterol change did not reach statistical significance, meaning that there was at least a 5 percent chance that the cholesterol rise could have been due to chance alone.","entailment":"The quote explains that the observed cholesterol change was not statistically significant, implying a ≥5% probability it was due to chance.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"cholesterol change","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"at least a 5 percent chance","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"In 2015, the U.S. Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee reported that “available evidence shows no appreciable relationship between consumption of dietary cholesterol and serum cholesterol … .”","claimType":"empirical","quote":"In 2015, the U.S. Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee reported that “available evidence shows no appreciable relationship between consumption of dietary cholesterol and serum cholesterol … .”","entailment":"The quote records the Committee’s statement that the evidence did not show an appreciable relationship between dietary cholesterol intake and serum cholesterol.","population":"","intervention":"consumption of dietary cholesterol","comparator":"","outcome":"serum cholesterol","timeframe":"2015","quantifiers":"no appreciable relationship","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"According to a 2019 meta-analysis, eating an egg each day raises low density lipoprotein (LDL, or “bad”) cholesterol by about nine points.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"According to a 2019 meta-analysis, eating an egg each day raises low density lipoprotein (LDL, or “bad”) cholesterol by about nine points.","entailment":"The quote reports the meta‑analysis finding that daily egg consumption increases LDL cholesterol by roughly nine units.","population":"","intervention":"eating an egg each day","comparator":"","outcome":"LDL cholesterol increase","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"about nine points","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Every 100 milligrams of added dietary cholesterol (approximately half an egg) raised LDL (“bad”) cholesterol levels by about 4.5 mg/dL.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Every 100 milligrams of added dietary cholesterol (approximately half an egg) raised LDL (“bad”) cholesterol levels by about 4.5 mg/dL.","entailment":"The quote quantifies the effect of 100 mg of added dietary cholesterol on LDL cholesterol, indicating an increase of about 4.5 mg/dL.","population":"","intervention":"100 milligrams of added dietary cholesterol (≈ half an egg)","comparator":"","outcome":"LDL cholesterol levels","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"about 4.5 mg/dL","modality":"causal"}]}
